_meta.primarySpecialty: Ophthalmology / Oculoplastic Surgery / Oral & Maxillofacial Surgery
_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAO BCSC Section 7 (Oculofacial Plastic and Orbital Surgery), AAOMS parameters of care for orbital reconstruction, Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery journal series on patient-specific implants, Ellis orbital fracture management algorithms, and Marin-Padilla craniofacial developmental biology. Volume thresholds (1.5 mL volume change = 1 mm enophthalmos) from Manson, Clauser, and Hammer biomechanical studies.

h1Title: Orbital Deformity: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
specialistType: Ophthalmology / Oculoplastic Surgery / Oral & Maxillofacial Surgery
heroOverview: Orbital deformity is a structural abnormality of the bony eye socket that alters globe position, eye motility, or facial symmetry. The pyramidal orbit is built from seven bones with a normal volume of 28-32 mL, and even a 1.5 mL volume gain produces 1 mm of measurable enophthalmos. Causes split into three groups: post-traumatic malunion of orbital fractures (the largest single cause in adults), congenital craniofacial syndromes such as Crouzon, Apert, and Treacher Collins (the dominant pediatric cause), and acquired conditions including fibrous dysplasia, neurofibromatosis type 1, post-tumor resection defects, post-radiation hypoplasia, and silent sinus syndrome. Patients present with facial asymmetry, sunken or protruding eye, double vision, and restricted gaze. Modern reconstruction with patient-specific 3D-printed titanium implants restores orbital volume within 1 mm of the unaffected side in over 90% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Post-traumatic orbital fractures occur in approximately 4-16% of all facial fractures; ~30% develop malunion-related deformity if untreated (Ellis 2012)
keyStats.demographics: Adult traumatic cases peak in males 20-40 years; congenital craniosynostosis syndromes affect ~1 in 25,000 (Crouzon) to ~1 in 50,000 (Treacher Collins) live births
keyStats.avgAge: Traumatic onset typically 20-40 years; syndromic deformities present in infancy; silent sinus syndrome typically presents 30-50 years
keyStats.globalCases: No unified registry; the WHO Global Burden of Disease lists facial fracture incidence at ~7-9 million per year, of which a meaningful fraction develops chronic deformity

definition: Orbital deformity (ICD-10: H05.3) is a structural abnormality of the bony orbit that disrupts the normal pyramidal architecture, alters orbital volume, or displaces the globe from its reference position. The orbit is formed by contributions from the frontal, zygomatic, maxillary, sphenoid, ethmoid, lacrimal, and palatine bones, enclosing roughly 28-32 mL of soft tissue including the globe, extraocular muscles, fat, vessels, and nerves. Any process that fractures, displaces, hypoplasies, hypertrophies, or resorbs these walls changes orbital volume and globe position. Clinically the condition encompasses post-traumatic malunion (most often after blowout fractures of the floor, medial wall fractures, naso-orbito-ethmoid disruption, zygomaticomaxillary complex shifts, and Le Fort II or III patterns), congenital deformity in craniofacial syndromes with cranial-suture fusion or branchial-arch maldevelopment, and acquired bony pathology such as fibrous dysplasia, neurofibromatous orbital dysplasia, post-resection or post-radiation hypoplasia, and silent sinus syndrome. Management is shared by oculoplastic surgeons, oral and maxillofacial surgeons, and craniofacial plastic surgeons, with neurosurgery and otolaryngology involved for syndromic and posterior-orbit cases.

typesClassification.description: The dominant adult etiology. Results from a healed but malreduced orbital fracture — blowout floor or medial wall fracture, naso-orbito-ethmoid disruption, zygomaticomaxillary complex displacement, or Le Fort II/III patterns. Volume expansion produces enophthalmos and hypoglobus; soft-tissue entrapment produces persistent diplopia.

Premature fusion of cranial sutures or branchial-arch maldevelopment produces shallow, hypoplastic, or laterally displaced orbits. Crouzon, Apert, and Pfeiffer syndromes share FGFR mutations and craniosynostosis with midface hypoplasia. Treacher Collins (TCOF1, POLR1C/D) produces hypoplastic zygomas and antimongoloid orbital slant.

Fibrous dysplasia replaces normal bone with disorganized fibro-osseous tissue and slowly expands orbital walls. Neurofibromatosis type 1 produces sphenoid wing dysplasia and pulsating proptosis. Post-tumor resection or post-radiation in childhood produces hypoplasia. Silent sinus syndrome — chronic maxillary sinus collapse — gradually depresses the orbital floor.

Unilateral coronal or lambdoid synostosis (or severe positional plagiocephaly) tilts the orbital rim and produces orbital dystopia — vertical displacement of one orbit relative to the other. Distinct from true syndromic forms; often correctable with cranial vault remodeling in infancy.

primarySymptoms: Visible facial asymmetry, with one side of the midface appearing flatter, narrower, or shorter than the other when viewed from above (the worm's-eye view photograph is diagnostic in clinic).

Enophthalmos — a sunken-eye appearance with deep upper-lid sulcus and apparent ptosis — measured on Hertel exophthalmometry as a difference greater than 2 mm between sides, indicating expanded orbital volume.
Exophthalmos or pulsating proptosis on the affected side, classically seen with sphenoid wing dysplasia of neurofibromatosis type 1 or expansile fibrous dysplasia.
Hypoglobus — vertical drop of the globe below the contralateral pupillary axis — visible as asymmetric lower-lid show and producing a downward-gaze deficit.
Binocular diplopia, especially on upgaze or lateral gaze, from inferior rectus or medial rectus entrapment in a healed fracture or from globe malposition altering muscle vector.

Telecanthus or orbital dystopia with widened intercanthal distance and vertical orbital displacement, characteristic of healed naso-orbito-ethmoid (NOE) fractures and severe syndromic craniosynostoses.
Acquired ptosis or pseudoptosis from disrupted levator anatomy or a posteriorly displaced globe pulling the upper lid downward.
Restricted extraocular movement, often most apparent on upgaze after floor fractures, with a positive forced-duction test indicating mechanical rather than neurogenic restriction.
Chronic ocular surface irritation, dry eye, and exposure keratopathy from impaired lid closure or lagophthalmos when the globe is displaced anteriorly or the lower lid is retracted.
Infraorbital hypoesthesia along the cheek, upper teeth, and lip — a sign of healed maxillary fracture with infraorbital nerve compression or stretch injury.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent diplopia, sunken eye, or facial asymmetry more than 6 weeks after an orbital injury — these signs predict malunion and warrant CT imaging before swelling fully resolves and the diagnosis becomes harder
Progressive proptosis or visible cheek flattening in a child with cafe-au-lait spots or known neurofibromatosis type 1, suggesting sphenoid wing dysplasia
Slowly increasing eye prominence with bony cheek expansion in a teenager or young adult, suggesting fibrous dysplasia of the orbit
Painless gradual sinking of the eye with deepening upper-lid sulcus and no history of trauma — silent sinus syndrome until proven otherwise
Unilateral or asymmetric orbital appearance in an infant with abnormal head shape — refer for craniofacial assessment before suture fusion is complete
emergencySigns: Acute vision loss, severe proptosis, and a tense eye after recent trauma — possible retrobulbar hemorrhage or orbital compartment syndrome requiring lateral canthotomy and cantholysis within minutes
Cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea or pneumocephalus after orbital roof or NOE fracture — needs urgent neurosurgical evaluation
Fixed dilated pupil, ophthalmoplegia, and vision loss in the setting of an orbital apex fracture — possible orbital apex syndrome

Worsening diplopia with nausea and bradycardia in a child after a floor fracture — the white-eyed blowout fracture with trapped inferior rectus muscle (oculocardiac reflex) requires surgical release within 24-48 hours

causes.description: The single largest cause of acquired orbital deformity. Blowout fractures of the floor and medial wall expand orbital volume and herniate fat and muscle into the maxillary or ethmoid sinus. Naso-orbito-ethmoid, zygomaticomaxillary complex, and Le Fort II/III patterns shift the orbital walls and rim, producing enophthalmos, hypoglobus, telecanthus, and diplopia when not anatomically reduced within 2 weeks.

Activating mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor genes (FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3) cause premature fusion of coronal and other sutures with midface and orbital hypoplasia. The orbits are shallow, exorbitism is severe enough to risk corneal exposure, and intracranial pressure may rise. Often diagnosed in infancy.

causes.cause: Treacher Collins syndrome and related mandibulofacial dysostoses
causes.description: TCOF1, POLR1C, and POLR1D mutations disrupt ribosomal biogenesis in cranial neural crest cells. The zygomatic complex and lateral orbital walls are hypoplastic or absent, producing antimongoloid slant, eyelid colobomas, and lateral orbital wall deficiency.

A sporadic GNAS post-zygotic mutation replaces normal bone with disorganized fibro-osseous tissue. Monostotic, polyostotic, and McCune-Albright variants exist. Slow expansion of the sphenoid, frontal, or maxillary contribution to the orbit produces proptosis, dystopia, and optic canal narrowing.

causes.cause: Neurofibromatosis type 1 with sphenoid wing dysplasia
causes.description: NF1 mutation produces absence or dysplasia of the greater wing of the sphenoid in 5-10% of patients. The temporal lobe herniates anteriorly, transmitting pulsations to the globe — the classic pulsating proptosis. Plexiform neurofibromas of the orbit add a soft-tissue component.

Chronic occlusion of the maxillary sinus ostium creates persistent negative pressure that gradually collapses the antrum and resorbs the orbital floor. Patients present with painless progressive enophthalmos and hypoglobus over months to years, typically in the third to fifth decade.

Retinoblastoma enucleation in infancy, rhabdomyosarcoma resection, or orbital radiation arrests bony growth on the affected side. The orbit remains smaller than the contralateral one through development, producing facial asymmetry and contracted socket in adulthood.

causes.cause: Inflammatory and granulomatous disease (IgG4-related disease, granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
causes.description: Chronic orbital inflammation in IgG4-related ophthalmic disease and ANCA-associated vasculitis can produce bony remodeling, sclerosis, and secondary deformity of the orbital walls and the lacrimal fossa over years.

riskFactors.description: Motor-vehicle collisions, assault, falls, and sports injuries account for most adult orbital fractures. Inadequate primary reduction within 2 weeks raises malunion risk roughly 3-4 fold.
Adult traumatic orbital fractures occur 3-4 times more often in men, peaking in the third decade, mirroring the demographics of facial trauma.
Autosomal dominant FGFR2/FGFR3 mutations are the basis of syndromic craniosynostosis with orbital hypoplasia; de novo mutations are common and paternal-age-related.
Cause Treacher Collins syndrome with bilateral lateral orbital wall hypoplasia, malar deficiency, and antimongoloid orbital slant.
Autosomal dominant; sphenoid wing dysplasia is one of the diagnostic criteria and produces pulsating proptosis.
Recurrent or chronic sinusitis with ostiomeatal obstruction predisposes to silent sinus syndrome. Endoscopic correction of obstruction may halt progression early.
Doses above 10-20 Gy arrest bony orbital growth; enucleation before age 7 produces severe contracted socket. Use of orbital expanders mitigates but does not eliminate the deficit.
Higher exposure to high-energy facial impact, projectile injury, and blast injury increases incidence of complex orbital fractures with greater malunion risk.

affectedDemographics: Adult traumatic cases: males outnumber females 3-4:1, peaking ages 20-40 (US National Trauma Data Bank)
Syndromic craniosynostosis is rare overall (~1 in 2,100 to 1 in 2,500 live births for all forms combined), with Crouzon at ~1 in 25,000 and Apert at ~1 in 65,000
Sphenoid wing dysplasia occurs in 5-10% of NF1 patients (NIH NF1 cohort data)
Silent sinus syndrome typically presents in the third to fifth decade with no clear sex predilection
Pediatric orbital fractures account for ~15% of facial fractures in children, with the trapdoor variant unique to growing bone
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical assessment with thin-slice imaging. Examination begins with standardized photography from front, profile, submental-vertex (worm's-eye), and bird's-eye views — these are the reference document for surgical planning and outcome assessment. Hertel exophthalmometry quantifies anterior-posterior globe position to within 0.5 mm, with normal mean values of 18-22 mm and pathologic asymmetry above 2 mm between sides. The cornea-to-pupil vertical line and intercanthal distance establish hypoglobus and telecanthus. Forced duction testing differentiates mechanical restriction (positive) from neurogenic palsy (negative). The decisive imaging study is a thin-slice (0.5-1 mm) non-contrast facial computed tomography scan with axial, coronal, and sagittal reformats; this remains the gold standard for bony orbital anatomy per AAO and AAOMS guidelines. Three-dimensional reconstruction from the CT dataset is used for surgical planning and to fabricate patient-specific implants by mirroring the unaffected orbit onto the affected side. Magnetic resonance imaging is added when soft-tissue assessment is needed — orbital tumor, plexiform neurofibroma, optic nerve compression, or active inflammation. Volume calculation software derives precise orbital volume from segmented CT data, with the contralateral orbit serving as the patient's internal reference. The most common error in initial trauma evaluation is reading the CT as 'minimally displaced' on early swollen images and missing volume expansion that only becomes apparent once edema resolves; repeat imaging at 7-14 days clarifies the true deformity.

diagnosticTests.test: Thin-slice facial CT with multiplanar reformats
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for bony orbital anatomy. Defines fracture location, fragment displacement, soft-tissue herniation, optic canal patency, and skull-base integrity. Quantifies orbital volume against the contralateral side.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contrast CT taking 5-10 minutes in the scanner. Axial, coronal, and sagittal views are reconstructed at 0.5-1 mm slices. Radiation dose is comparable to a head CT. Results read same-day in most centers.

diagnosticTests.test: 3D CT reconstruction with mirror-image surgical planning
diagnosticTests.purpose: Builds a virtual model of both orbits, mirrors the unaffected side onto the affected side, and identifies the corrective volume and shape needed. Used to design patient-specific 3D-printed titanium implants.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: No additional scan is needed; the reconstruction is generated from the existing CT dataset by the surgical team or implant vendor. Adds 1-2 weeks of planning time before surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates extraocular muscles, optic nerve, orbital fat, neurofibromas, IgG4 inflammatory mass, and any intracranial component. Adds value when a soft-tissue pathology drives the deformity or when planning syndromic reconstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minute scan in the MRI machine. No radiation. Contrast (gadolinium) may be given through an IV line. Earplugs are provided for noise.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies anterior-posterior globe position. Differences greater than 2 mm between sides indicate clinically significant enophthalmos or proptosis and guide the need for volume restoration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small handheld device is rested on both lateral orbital rims; the patient looks forward. Reading takes under 2 minutes; painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents facial asymmetry pre-operatively and at each follow-up. Front, lateral profile, submental-vertex (worm's-eye view), and bird's-eye view are the minimum set. Essential for surgical planning and outcome audit.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Series of photographs taken with consistent lighting, distance, and head position in clinic. Takes 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates mechanical entrapment (positive) from neurogenic ophthalmoplegia (negative) when extraocular movement is restricted. Performed under topical or local anesthesia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After numbing drops, a fine forceps gently moves the globe through its range of motion. Tightness suggests mechanical restriction. Takes 5-10 minutes in the office or under sedation in children.

diagnosticTests.test: Visual function assessment (visual acuity, color vision, visual fields, pupil reaction)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects optic nerve compromise from optic canal narrowing in fibrous dysplasia, apex fracture, or syndromic synostosis. Required baseline before any orbital reconstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard ophthalmic examination with chart vision, Ishihara color plates, automated perimetry, and pupil testing. Takes 30-45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Management of orbital deformity is staged and multidisciplinary. The first decision is timing: acute orbital fractures with entrapment, white-eyed pediatric blowout, large volume expansion, early enophthalmos, or visual compromise are repaired within days; clean isolated fractures without functional deficit can be observed for 1-2 weeks while edema resolves, then repaired or watched. Once deformity has consolidated past 4-6 weeks, reconstruction shifts from simple fracture reduction to formal volume restoration with implant. The reconstructive workhorses are anatomically pre-shaped titanium mesh, porous polyethylene with embedded titanium, and — increasingly — patient-specific 3D-printed titanium implants designed from mirror-image CT data. Patient-specific implants restore orbital volume within 1 mm of the unaffected side in over 90% of cases and shorten operative time. Approach is most often transconjunctival with or without lateral canthotomy for the floor and medial wall; the transcaruncular approach gives access to the medial wall; coronal flap is used for complex panfacial and syndromic cases. Severe syndromic deformity requires Le Fort III osteotomy with midface advancement (Tessier), monobloc frontofacial advancement, or facial bipartition; these are typically staged in childhood and adolescence by craniofacial teams. Fibrous dysplasia is contoured by burring or shaving; aggressive optic canal involvement may need decompression to protect vision. Neurofibromatosis sphenoid wing dysplasia is reconstructed with split calvarial bone graft and titanium plating. Silent sinus syndrome is treated with endoscopic uncinectomy and middle meatal antrostomy plus orbital floor implant when enophthalmos is established. Adjunctive fat grafting (Coleman technique) corrects residual volume deficit and lid sulcus depression after bony reconstruction has set the framework. Anatomic restoration is the goal — perfect symmetry is rarely achievable but functional vision, single binocular vision in primary gaze, and acceptable facial appearance are reached in the great majority of contemporary repairs.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation with serial photography and Hertel readings
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for stable, mild deformity without functional deficit (no diplopia in primary or reading gaze, no exposure, no progression). Examined at 3, 6, and 12 months with standardized photographs and exophthalmometry. Surgery deferred unless deformity progresses or function declines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acceptable in roughly 20-30% of mild post-traumatic cases; the majority of moderate-to-severe deformities ultimately need reconstruction.

medicalTreatments.name: Prism spectacles for residual diplopia
medicalTreatments.description: Fresnel or ground-in prisms compensate for stable small-angle diplopia in primary gaze when the patient is not a surgical candidate or has residual diplopia after reconstruction. Prescribed by the ophthalmologist or orthoptist after motility has stabilized for at least 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single binocular vision in primary and reading gaze achieved in 60-80% of patients with small fusion-amenable deviations.

medicalTreatments.name: Lubricant drops, ointment, and moisture chambers for exposure
medicalTreatments.description: Preservative-free artificial tears every 1-2 hours, lubricant ointment at night, and overnight moisture chambers protect the cornea while bony reconstruction is being planned or in residual exposure after repair.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents exposure keratopathy in the majority of patients when adherence is good; not a substitute for definitive surgical correction.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin chemodenervation of the antagonist muscle
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct for persistent diplopia from a restrictive muscle by temporarily weakening its functional antagonist (typically lateral rectus when inferior rectus is restricted). Effect lasts 3-4 months and is repeated as needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful bridge therapy and diagnostic test; 40-60% achieve sustained alignment without further strabismus surgery in selected cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonates for symptomatic fibrous dysplasia
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous pamidronate or zoledronate reduces bone pain and slows lesion growth in polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. Does not reverse established deformity but may delay surgery and stabilize disease activity in growing patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain scores in 60-70% of treated patients (Plotkin 2003 and subsequent series); modest effect on lesion volume.

medicalTreatments.name: Glucocorticoids and steroid-sparing immunosuppression for IgG4-related orbital disease
medicalTreatments.description: Oral prednisolone induces remission in active IgG4-related ophthalmic disease; rituximab is used for relapsing or steroid-dependent disease. Treating the inflammation halts ongoing bony remodeling, after which any residual deformity is reconstructed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial response to corticosteroids in over 80% of patients; rituximab achieves remission in 70-80% of refractory cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital reconstruction with titanium mesh or porous polyethylene implant
surgicalOptions.description: Standard contemporary repair for floor and medial wall defects. Pre-shaped or hand-contoured implant is placed through a transconjunctival, transcaruncular, or subciliary approach to restore the bony orbital contour. Suitable for acute repair within 2 weeks and for delayed repair up to 6 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restores orbital volume within 2 mL of contralateral side and resolves diplopia in primary gaze in 80-90% of patients; persistent diplopia rate 5-10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Patient-specific 3D-printed titanium implant
surgicalOptions.description: Designed pre-operatively from mirror-image CT data and printed in selective laser-melted titanium. Particularly valuable for complex, large, or revision defects and for syndromic asymmetry. Reduces operating time and improves anatomic accuracy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restores orbital volume within 1 mL of the unaffected side in over 90% of cases; resolves enophthalmos to under 1 mm asymmetry in over 85% (Mommaerts and successor series).

surgicalOptions.name: Le Fort III osteotomy with midface advancement
surgicalOptions.description: Tessier's classical operation for syndromic midface and orbital hypoplasia. The midface block including the zygomas, maxilla, and nasal complex is mobilized and advanced. Distraction osteogenesis is used in many modern protocols for more controlled gradual advancement, especially in growing children.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves clinically acceptable orbital depth and dental occlusion in 80-90% of pediatric syndromic cases; staged revisions sometimes required at skeletal maturity.

surgicalOptions.name: Monobloc frontofacial advancement and facial bipartition
surgicalOptions.description: For severe Crouzon, Apert, or Pfeiffer phenotypes the frontal bone and midface are advanced as one block (monobloc) or split sagittally to correct orbital hypertelorism (bipartition). Performed by combined neurosurgery and craniofacial plastic surgery teams.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves orbital depth, airway, and intracranial pressure; major morbidity (CSF leak, infection, vision loss) 5-10% in modern series — appropriate only at experienced craniofacial centers.

surgicalOptions.name: Autologous bone grafting (calvarial, iliac, rib)
surgicalOptions.description: Split-calvarial bone graft remains first-line for reconstructing the absent greater sphenoid wing in NF1, large orbital roof defects, and revision of failed alloplastic implants. Iliac and costochondral grafts are used for larger volume defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term graft viability over 80%; some resorption (10-30%) is expected and accounted for at the time of placement.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic uncinectomy with middle meatal antrostomy and orbital floor reconstruction (silent sinus syndrome)
surgicalOptions.description: Restores maxillary sinus ventilation and stops further floor depression. When enophthalmos and hypoglobus are already established, the orbital floor is reconstructed at the same operation or in a second stage with implant.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves enophthalmos to under 1 mm asymmetry in 80-90% of cases when combined with floor reconstruction.

surgicalOptions.name: Autologous fat grafting (Coleman technique)
surgicalOptions.description: Adjunct for residual volume deficit, deep upper-lid sulcus, and contour irregularity after bony reconstruction. Fat harvested from the abdomen or thigh is purified and injected in submicroliter aliquots into the deficient soft-tissue compartments.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sustained graft survival 50-70% per session; often repeated once or twice to reach target contour.

surgicalOptions.name: Strabismus surgery for residual diplopia
surgicalOptions.description: Recession or resection of restrictive extraocular muscles after bony reconstruction has stabilized and motility has plateaued. Performed by a strabismus specialist 6 months or more after the orbital repair.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Single binocular vision in primary and reading gaze achieved in 60-80% of patients with persistent post-reconstruction diplopia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vision therapy and orthoptic exercises
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured exercises supervised by an orthoptist can extend fusion range for small residual deviations and reduce reliance on prism correction. Effective only when sensory fusion is intact and the deviation is small.

alternativeTreatments.name: Camouflage cosmetics and lash extensions
alternativeTreatments.description: For mild residual asymmetry that does not justify further surgery, cosmetic adjustments (eyebrow shaping, eyelash extensions, lid-shadow contouring) can soften visible asymmetry. Adjunctive only; does not alter underlying anatomy.

whySeeSpecialist: An oculoplastic surgeon, oral and maxillofacial surgeon, or craniofacial plastic surgeon should evaluate any patient with persistent diplopia, measurable enophthalmos or proptosis greater than 2 mm, facial asymmetry, or syndromic features. Sub-specialty training matters: a complex post-traumatic malunion, fibrous dysplasia with optic canal involvement, or syndromic craniofacial deformity is not appropriate for a general plastic surgery or ENT practice without orbital experience. Pediatric syndromic cases belong in formal craniofacial centers with combined plastic, neurosurgical, ophthalmologic, and dental teams.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship training in oculofacial plastic surgery (ASOPRS), craniomaxillofacial surgery (AO-CMF or AAOMS), or pediatric craniofacial surgery, with documented experience in patient-specific implant planning and 3D virtual surgical workflows. Ask about volume — at least 25-50 orbital reconstructions per year is a reasonable threshold for non-syndromic cases, and craniofacial centers should perform multiple Le Fort III or monobloc procedures annually. Multidisciplinary tumor boards or craniofacial clinics indicate appropriate team-based care.
hospitalCriteria: Access to a high-resolution facial CT scanner (≤1 mm slices) and a radiology team experienced in 3D reconstruction
On-site or contracted virtual surgical planning service and patient-specific implant fabrication
Operating microscope, neuronavigation, and intraoperative CT or fluoroscopy for complex orbital and craniofacial work
Same-admission ophthalmology consult availability for visual function monitoring
ICU step-down capacity for monobloc and Le Fort III cases with CSF-leak surveillance
keyFacilities: Major academic medical centers with combined oculoplastic, oral and maxillofacial, and craniofacial plastic surgery teams
Children's hospitals with dedicated craniofacial programs for syndromic synostosis
Level 1 trauma centers for acute orbital fracture repair
Specialist neurofibromatosis clinics for complex NF1 orbital and sphenoid wing disease
preventionStrategies: Wear certified protective eyewear (ANSI Z87.1 or equivalent) for high-risk sports, woodworking, metalwork, and shooting — projectile and blunt-impact eye injury is the most preventable cause of orbital fracture
Use seatbelts and airbags and avoid distracted driving — motor vehicle collisions remain the leading cause of severe panfacial fractures in adults
Repair displaced orbital fractures within 2 weeks of injury where surgically indicated — delayed primary repair raises malunion risk 3-4 fold
Treat chronic maxillary sinus disease early with endoscopic correction of ostiomeatal obstruction to reduce the risk of silent sinus syndrome
In children with craniosynostosis, refer to a craniofacial centre before age 1 — early cranial vault remodeling preserves orbital and brain growth potential
lifestyleModifications: Use preservative-free lubricant drops every 1-2 hours during the day and lubricant ointment at night while corneal exposure persists
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated 30 degrees in the first 6 weeks after orbital reconstruction to limit periorbital swelling
Avoid nose-blowing for 4 weeks after orbital floor or medial wall reconstruction to prevent orbital emphysema
Wear protective eyewear in the operated eye for 3 months after surgery and indefinitely during high-risk activities
Stop tobacco use at least 4 weeks before and after any major reconstructive surgery — nicotine impairs bone graft and soft-tissue healing
Maintain a soft diet for 4-6 weeks after Le Fort osteotomies to protect the osteotomy lines

recommended: Protein 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day during the early postoperative period to support bone and soft-tissue healing
Vitamin D (800-1000 IU daily) and calcium (1000-1200 mg daily) to support bony remodeling, especially in fibrous dysplasia
Iron-rich foods and vitamin C to support hemoglobin recovery after surgery with significant blood loss
Adequate hydration (2 L daily) to support tissue perfusion and graft viability
Anti-inflammatory diet emphasizing fish, olive oil, vegetables, and fruits during long recovery courses
avoid: Hard, crunchy, or chewy foods for 4-6 weeks after Le Fort osteotomy or large mid-face procedures
Excess alcohol in the perioperative window — interferes with healing and increases bleeding risk
Tobacco and nicotine products in any form before and after surgery
Foods that require prolonged forceful chewing in the early healing phase
High-dose vitamin E and fish-oil supplements in the week before surgery — both increase bleeding risk

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid strenuous exercise, bending, lifting more than 10 kg, and contact sports for 4-6 weeks after orbital reconstruction. Light walking is encouraged from day 2 to reduce thromboembolic risk. Return to non-contact aerobic exercise at 4 weeks, full strength training at 6 weeks, and contact sports only after 12 weeks and surgeon clearance — and always with appropriate protective eyewear.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed lubricant drops and ointment on schedule until corneal coverage normalizes
Photograph the face weekly in the first 3 months to document symmetry and detect any regression in volume
Attend follow-up at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months after reconstruction; CT or MRI is repeated at 3-6 months when there is concern about implant position
Report new or worsening diplopia, vision change, pain, or asymmetry promptly — implant migration or hematoma can be remediable if caught early
Maintain dental and sinus hygiene to limit infection risk to the implant
prognosis: With modern reconstruction the functional and aesthetic outlook is good. Patient-specific titanium implants restore orbital volume within 1 mm of the unaffected side in over 90% of cases and resolve diplopia in primary and reading gaze in 80-90% of patients (Mommaerts, Strong, and successor series). Residual mild enophthalmos under 2 mm is common, cosmetically subtle, and acceptable to most patients. The strongest negative prognostic factors are repair more than 6 months after injury, multi-wall (floor plus medial) defects, large defects greater than 2 cm in any dimension, and prior failed reconstruction. Syndromic craniofacial deformities require staged operations through childhood and adolescence and typically achieve close-to-normal facial proportions and binocular function by skeletal maturity, with some patients requiring revision into early adulthood. Fibrous dysplasia is a slowly progressive condition; surveillance of the optic canal is lifelong, and vision is preserved in over 90% with timely decompression when indicated. NF1 sphenoid wing reconstruction has higher revision rates (20-30%) due to dural pulsation and graft resorption but restores acceptable globe stability in most patients.
recoveryTimeline: Most periorbital swelling settles within 3-4 weeks of orbital reconstruction; bruising fades by week 6. Diplopia present immediately after surgery typically resolves over 6-12 weeks as edema clears and the muscle re-finds its vector; persistent diplopia at 6 months is unlikely to resolve without further intervention. Bone graft incorporation takes 3-6 months; titanium implant osseointegration is functional within 6 weeks. Return to office work is usual at 1-2 weeks for isolated orbital floor cases, 4-6 weeks for Le Fort or panfacial cases. Final aesthetic and functional plateau is reached by 6-12 months.
complications: Persistent diplopia from incompletely released entrapment, muscle scarring, or implant malposition — present in 5-10% of repairs and often improves with strabismus surgery
Residual enophthalmos greater than 2 mm from incomplete volume restoration — addressed with secondary implant exchange or fat grafting
Implant migration, infection, or extrusion — rare with titanium (under 2%) but higher with porous polyethylene (3-5%) and uncommon nonresorbable materials
Corneal exposure and chronic dry eye from lid retraction, lagophthalmos, or globe malposition
Optic neuropathy from over-aggressive posterior dissection, retrobulbar hematoma, or compression by a misplaced implant — vision-threatening, ranges from transient to permanent in 0.5-2% of cases
Cerebrospinal fluid leak after roof, sphenoid, or monobloc procedures — usually settles with bed rest or lumbar drainage; rarely needs revision
Infraorbital nerve hypoesthesia or neuralgia after floor and zygomatic reconstruction — usually improves over 6-12 months
Graft resorption and recurrent deformity in fibrous dysplasia or NF1 reconstruction, requiring revision over months to years

supportResources.description: Directory of fellowship-trained oculoplastic surgeons in North America.

supportResources.name: Children's Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: Family support, education, and advocacy for children and families affected by Crouzon, Apert, Pfeiffer, Treacher Collins and related syndromes.

supportResources.name: Fibrous Dysplasia Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient organization for fibrous dysplasia and McCune-Albright syndrome with clinical and research resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Enophthalmos (Sunken Eye)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Enophthalmos is the clinical finding of a posteriorly displaced globe (greater than 2 mm asymmetry on Hertel exophthalmometry), not a diagnosis. Orbital deformity describes the underlying bony architecture that produces it. Most enophthalmos is caused by orbital deformity, but it can also follow soft-tissue fat atrophy, scleroderma-like fibrosing disease, or congenital microphthalmos with normal bony orbits.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thyroid Eye Disease (Graves' Orbitopathy)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Graves' orbitopathy produces bilateral or asymmetric proptosis from enlarged extraocular muscles and increased orbital fat — the bony orbit is structurally normal. Thyroid antibodies are positive, lid retraction is common, and CT shows muscle belly thickening with normal tendons. Orbital deformity from H05.3 by contrast shows abnormal bony architecture on CT with normal muscle morphology unless entrapped.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic sinusitis is mucosal inflammation of the paranasal sinuses without altered orbital bony anatomy in most cases. When chronic maxillary obstruction progresses to silent sinus syndrome, the orbital floor depresses and the condition crosses into orbital deformity. Standard chronic sinusitis without this collapse produces facial pain and nasal symptoms but a normal orbital floor on CT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Craniosynostosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Craniosynostosis is the underlying premature suture fusion that produces abnormal skull shape; orbital deformity is one of its downstream consequences in syndromic forms (Crouzon, Apert, Pfeiffer). A child can have non-syndromic single-suture craniosynostosis with only mild orbital tilt, while orbital deformity from trauma occurs in adults with normal cranial sutures. The two diagnoses can coexist or stand alone.

relatedConditions.name: Enophthalmos
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common clinical manifestation of orbital deformity; together they describe the bony cause and the visible globe-position finding.

relatedConditions.name: Craniosynostosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Syndromic craniosynostosis is one of the major congenital causes of orbital deformity, sharing management between craniofacial and oculoplastic teams.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism / Thyroid Eye Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential for proptosis and lid retraction; soft-tissue rather than bony deformity but commonly co-evaluated.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic ostiomeatal obstruction underlies silent sinus syndrome, an acquired cause of orbital deformity.

relatedConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with naso-orbito-ethmoid fractures and complicates surgical access through the medial orbital wall.

relatedConditions.name: Bell's Palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Facial nerve weakness with poor lid closure compounds exposure keratopathy when the globe is anteriorly displaced in orbital deformity.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Altered orbital architecture and globe position influence intraocular pressure measurement and surgical planning when both conditions coexist.

faqs.question: What is orbital deformity?
faqs.answer: Orbital deformity (ICD-10 H05.3) is a structural abnormality of the bony eye socket that changes its volume or shape and displaces the eye. Causes are congenital syndromes, healed orbital fractures, or acquired bony disease.

faqs.question: What causes orbital deformity in adults?
faqs.answer: The most common adult cause is malunion of an orbital fracture — blowout, naso-orbito-ethmoid, zygomaticomaxillary complex, or Le Fort II/III. Other causes include fibrous dysplasia, NF1 sphenoid wing dysplasia, post-tumor or post-radiation hypoplasia, and silent sinus syndrome.

faqs.question: What does orbital deformity look like?
faqs.answer: Classic findings are facial asymmetry, a sunken eye (enophthalmos) with deepened upper-lid crease, vertical drop of one eye below the other (hypoglobus), or a forward-pushed eye. Double vision on upgaze and restricted eye movement are common functional clues.

faqs.question: How is orbital deformity diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines standardized facial photography, Hertel exophthalmometry, and thin-slice facial CT scanning. CT defines bony anatomy and quantifies orbital volume; 3D reconstruction guides surgical planning. MRI is added when soft tissue or optic nerve needs assessment.

faqs.question: Can orbital deformity be fixed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most post-traumatic and acquired deformities are correctable with orbital reconstruction using titanium mesh, porous polyethylene, or patient-specific 3D-printed implants. Modern reconstruction restores orbital volume within 1 mm of the unaffected side in over 90% of cases.

faqs.question: What is a patient-specific orbital implant?
faqs.answer: A patient-specific implant is a titanium plate custom-designed from the patient's CT scan by mirroring the unaffected orbit onto the affected side, then 3D-printed in selective laser-melted titanium. It improves volume restoration and shortens operating time compared with hand-bent mesh.

faqs.question: When should an orbital fracture be repaired?
faqs.answer: Early repair within days is indicated for white-eyed pediatric blowout with muscle entrapment, fractures with over 2 cm of wall loss, early enophthalmos above 2 mm, or persistent diplopia in primary gaze. Other isolated fractures may be observed 1-2 weeks while edema settles.

faqs.question: What is silent sinus syndrome?
faqs.answer: Silent sinus syndrome is chronic collapse of the maxillary sinus from long-standing ostiomeatal obstruction. Persistent negative pressure pulls the orbital floor downward over months, producing painless progressive enophthalmos. Treatment is endoscopic sinus surgery plus floor reconstruction.

faqs.question: How is syndromic craniofacial deformity treated?
faqs.answer: Syndromic deformity from Crouzon, Apert, Pfeiffer, or Treacher Collins is staged through childhood. Steps include cranial vault remodeling in infancy, Le Fort III or midface distraction in childhood, monobloc or facial bipartition for severe deficiency, and orthognathic refinement at maturity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between enophthalmos and orbital deformity?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos is the clinical finding of a posteriorly displaced globe (over 2 mm asymmetry on Hertel). Orbital deformity is the underlying bony abnormality that produces it — for example, expanded orbital volume after a blowout fracture. Enophthalmos is a sign; orbital deformity is the diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is orbital deformity different from thyroid eye disease?
faqs.answer: Thyroid eye disease is a soft-tissue process — enlarged extraocular muscles and increased orbital fat — with structurally normal bony orbits. Orbital deformity is defined by abnormal bony architecture on CT. Thyroid antibodies, lid retraction, and the CT muscle pattern separate the two.

faqs.question: Does orbital deformity cause vision loss?
faqs.answer: Most do not threaten vision, but optic neuropathy can occur when the optic canal narrows from fibrous dysplasia, sphenoid wing dysplasia, or apex fracture, or when an implant or hematoma compresses the nerve. Visual function testing is part of every preoperative and postoperative assessment.

faqs.question: Can dry eye come from orbital deformity?
faqs.answer: Yes. When the globe sits forward or the lower lid is retracted, lids cannot close fully, exposing the cornea. Patients develop burning, foreign-body sensation, and exposure keratopathy. Lubricant drops help; definitive treatment is bony reconstruction plus lid procedures when needed.

faqs.question: Is orbital reconstruction painful?
faqs.answer: Orbital reconstruction is performed under general anesthesia. Postoperative discomfort is moderate for 3-5 days and well controlled with oral analgesics. Swelling and bruising peak at 48-72 hours and settle over 3-4 weeks. Office work resumes at 1-2 weeks for isolated repairs.

faqs.question: What are the risks of orbital reconstruction?
faqs.answer: Risks include persistent diplopia (5-10%), residual enophthalmos needing revision (5-10%), implant migration or infection (under 2% for titanium), corneal exposure, retrobulbar hematoma, and optic neuropathy (0.5-2%). Risk is higher with delayed repair and large multi-wall defects.

faqs.question: Can fat grafting alone fix orbital deformity?
faqs.answer: Fat grafting alone suits mild residual volume deficit and contour correction after bony reconstruction sets the framework. It cannot correct significant bony malposition, restore wall integrity, or release entrapped muscle. Coleman technique grafts achieve 50-70% survival per session.

faqs.question: Are children with craniofacial syndromes treated differently?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric syndromic patients are managed by combined craniofacial teams in staged operations that respect ongoing growth. Sequence is cranial vault remodeling in infancy, midface advancement in childhood, monobloc or Le Fort III later, and orthognathic refinement at skeletal maturity.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from orbital surgery take?
faqs.answer: Periorbital swelling settles in 3-4 weeks, bruising fades by week 6, and diplopia clears over 6-12 weeks. Office work resumes at 1-2 weeks for isolated repairs and 4-6 weeks for Le Fort and panfacial cases. Final functional plateau is reached by 6-12 months.

faqs.question: Will my eye look exactly the same after surgery?
faqs.answer: Perfect symmetry is rarely achievable. Most contemporary reconstructions restore globe position to within 1-2 mm of the unaffected side — cosmetically subtle and acceptable to most patients. Functional outcomes (single binocular vision, lid closure, full visual acuity) are the primary goals.

faqs.question: Does insurance cover orbital reconstruction?
faqs.answer: Reconstruction for functional indications (diplopia, exposure, vision threat, fracture repair, syndromic correction) is typically covered as medically necessary. Aesthetic refinement of mild residual asymmetry may be classed as cosmetic and not covered. Surgical teams usually pre-authorize.

faqs.question: What kind of doctor treats orbital deformity?
faqs.answer: Orbital deformity is shared by oculoplastic surgeons (ASOPRS), oral and maxillofacial surgeons, and craniofacial plastic surgeons. Complex syndromic and pediatric cases belong in craniofacial centers with neurosurgery, ENT, and dental teams. Fellowship training matters more than parent specialty.
